name: Residential Monitoring Cadence Optimization
consequences: Immediate: The tiered monitoring cadence reduces intrusiveness during nightly assessments, preserving participant comfort → Systemic: Over the pilot period, the reduced intrusiveness lowers event-capture rate by up to 20% and reduces false alarm incidence by 15%, signaling a trade-off between privacy and data richness → Strategic: This balance prioritizes ethical feasibility and recruitment stability, but may delay attainment of Aim 2 phenotyping milestones and require extended data collection windows.
options: Maintain current nightly low-burden monitoring across the cohort with predefined escalation thresholds and targeted full PSG nights only if flagged events occur.
Scale to include scheduled enhanced nights for all participants while implementing automated artifact detection and review triage to reduce technician workload.
Adopt a cloud-enabled, algorithm-assisted event triage layer with a shared-cost funding model that enables continuous escalation nights across partner clinics.
review: Controls privacy vs data richness. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-site standardization and GDPR constraints for multi-site deployment.

name: Data Governance and Privacy Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Strengthen data governance with de-identification and restricted cross-border access to minimize risk → Systemic: Cross-border transfer exposure declines by 90% but external collaboration slows by ~25%, reflecting a trade-off between governance and collaboration velocity → Strategic: Supports long-term compliance and trust at the cost of rapid external validation and multi-site harmonization.
options: Tighten data access with end-to-end de-identification and restricted cross-border sharing to minimize risk.
Adopt modular de-identification with controlled data releases to collaboration partners under data use agreements and anonymized subset sharing.
Create a consent-managed, tiered-access data platform with patient-advised governance and external researchers under a formal data governance framework.
review: Controls regulatory compliance vs data accessibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider operational burden and cost of ongoing de-identification processes.

name: Phenotyping and Event-Triage Tools
consequences: Immediate: Implement semi-automated event triage to pre-classify candidate events for human adjudication → Systemic: Reduces reviewer workload by ~30% and shortens initial scoring cycle from 48 hours to 24 hours, but may bias pre-classification toward more common phenotypes → Strategic: Increases throughput while preserving adjudication accuracy, enabling larger cohorts but requiring ongoing calibration to mitigate phenotype bias.
options: Maintain manual scoring with formal inter-rater reliability targets and established AASM adjudication protocol.
Introduce semi-automated event triage using computational scoring to pre-classify events for human adjudication and reduce review time.
Develop and deploy a scalable algorithm-assisted triage suite integrated with all sensor streams, run under a cross-site data-use framework with external collaborators.
review: Controls speed vs accuracy of adjudication. Weakness: The options fail to consider algorithm bias and misclassification risk for rare parasomnia subtypes.

name: Recruitment and Retention Optimization
consequences: Immediate: Maintain Bonn-region outreach through sleep clinics and patient registries while preserving safety checks → Systemic: Increases local recruitment by 10–15% but concentrates sample demographically, reducing geographic representativeness → Strategic: Improves feasibility and retention within the region at the cost of broader generalizability.
options: Maintain Bonn-region outreach through sleep clinics and patient registries while preserving safety checks.
Expand recruitment to national sleep clinics and broaden inclusion criteria to improve event yield while ensuring regulatory compliance.
Launch a participation-incentive program and a patient-advisory network to accelerate enrollment within a two-tier consent framework.
review: Controls recruitment yield vs representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural diversity and willingness to participate across non-Bonn populations.

name: Staffing and Safety Model Evolution
consequences: Immediate: Maintain 1:1 night coverage to ensure safety → Systemic: Preserves safety margins but raises staffing costs by ~15%, reducing budget flexibility and long-term scalability → Strategic: Maintains risk controls at the expense of agility and cost efficiency.
options: Preserve 1:1 night coverage to ensure safety.
Cross-train technicians for multiple duties and implement fatigue management to enable flexible night coverage.
Introduce robot-assisted safety checks and a flexible, outcome-based staffing consortium to optimize coverage and reduce burnout.
review: Controls safety coverage vs staffing costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider capital cost and maintenance of robot-assisted systems and regulatory acceptance.

name: Funding Innovation and Partnerships Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Pursue core DFG and University of Bonn funding with milestone-driven budgets and no dependence on industry → Systemic: Yields stable, investigator-led direction and predictable cash flow but constrains rapid scale and external validation → Strategic: Preserves academic independence at the cost of slower expansion and fewer immediate translational opportunities.
options: Pursue core DFG and University of Bonn funding with milestone-driven budgets and no dependence on industry.
Negotiate milestone-based funding from pharma partners with clearly defined data-use terms and a 30-day pre-publication window.
Establish a data accelerator consortium that monetizes de-identified datasets through tiered access and sponsor-initiated studies, reinvesting proceeds into expansion.
review: Controls independence vs reliance on external funding. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential conflicts of interest and publication rights in pharma partnerships.

name: Longitudinal Data Capture Protocol Harmonization
consequences: Immediate: Data fidelity and interoperability at the point of capture are enhanced through standardized metadata and synchronized timing across nights. Systemic: Cross-night comparability improves by 12% and annotation throughput increases by 20%, but the standardization raises upfront setup and training costs by €120k, trading consistency for agility. Strategic: Enables robust longitudinal phenotyping and accelerates multi-site collaboration, but requires ongoing governance to manage increased complexity and budgeting pressures.
options: Maintain current data capture protocols across suites with minor firmware updates to sensors and stricter annotation checklists
Introduce a harmonized EEG-BIDS metadata schema across nights and participants to improve cross-case comparability and reduce annotation ambiguity
Adopt a unified, on-site data fabric integrating edge-processing, automated anomaly detection, and live privacy-preserving data sharing to enable scalable cross-site collaboration
review: Controls data consistency vs. protocol flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider recruitment delays due to strict standardization.

name: In-Residence Safety and Environmental Design
consequences: Immediate: Safety incidents decline by 25% per participant-night due to reinforced design and protocols. Systemic: Incident rate drops but upfront capital expenditure increases by €500k and planning cycles lengthen by ~6 months, trading safety gains for budget and schedule. Strategic: A scalable safety-first environment enhances external partnerships and reputation, but requires sustainable funding and governance to avoid cost overruns.
options: Maintain current safety protocols with incremental environmental tweaks and staff training
Implement targeted architectural adjustments to reduce wandering risk while preserving domestic ambiance
Redesign living environment around modular, safety-first suites with embedded edge-sensing networks and a facility-as-a-service operating model, monetizing capacity through partner research programs
review: Controls safety enhancements vs domestic ambiance. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential disruption to patient experience and staff workflow.

name: Privacy-First Data Ecosystem and Consent Framework
consequences: Immediate: Data sharing viability improves through stronger privacy controls and clearer consent pathways. Systemic: External researcher access increases by 40%, but ongoing compliance and audit costs rise by €100k; trade-off openness against regulatory burden. Strategic: Enables scalable external collaborations and increases data assets for publications, but requires ongoing governance and legal oversight.
options: Maintain current de-identification protocols and access controls, with quarterly reviews
Enhance consent workflows and privacy-preserving data de-identification, enabling broader—but controlled—data sharing
Implement privacy-preserving on-site data fabric with differential privacy, secure multi-party computation, and a subscription-based external research access model
review: Controls openness of data sharing vs privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border consent challenges and data-transfer restrictions.

name: Talent Development and Night-Shift Sustainability
consequences: Immediate: Night-shift coverage reliability improves from 94% to 98%. Systemic: Overhead costs rise by €180k over three years and turnover declines by 10–15%, trading budgetary pressure for improved resilience. Strategic: At scale, program resilience increases and reduces research delays, but increases dependence on external talent networks and requires governance to prevent misalignment with institutional policies.
options: Maintain current staffing plan with incremental cross-training and retention incentives
Introduce structured career pathways, sabbaticals, and targeted recruitment drives to broaden talent pool
Establish a cross-institutional residency network with machine-learning-driven scheduling and a shared-operations service funded through a subscription model
review: Controls staffing reliability vs cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider staff burnout risk due to high demand.

name: Automated Scoring and Annotation Acceleration
consequences: Immediate: Annotation throughput increases by 30%, reducing manual review time. Systemic: Labor costs decrease by €120k, but false positives necessitate QC, trading speed for accuracy. Strategic: Speeds up publications and improves data quality but requires robust governance of automated decisions and transparent reporting of metrics.
options: Continue dual human scoring with existing tools and limited automation
Increase automation in annotation using machine-learning-assisted scoring with human verification
Deploy end-to-end automated scoring pipeline powered by edge-enabled machine-learning models, with real-time adjudication and a subscription-based external research access model
review: Controls automation throughput vs quality assurance. Weakness: The options fail to consider false-positive risk and the need for ongoing QC.

name: Sustainable Partnerships and Funding Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Funding stability increases via diversification. Systemic: Licensing revenues target €300k over three years, while IP management adds governance overhead and potential misalignment with academic priorities. Strategic: Enables scale and translational impact but requires ongoing IP governance and alignment with institutional policies.
options: Maintain existing funding mix and pursue incremental grants
Diversify funding through additional internal Bonn funds and selective pharma collaborations with predefined publication rights
Create an industry-academia consortium with milestone-based funding, shared-IP arrangements, and a licensing program for validated methods
review: Controls funding diversity vs IP control. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential conflicts with university IP policy and publication rights.

name: Privacy-centric Data Stewardship and External Collaboration
consequences: Immediate: Privacy safeguards reduce the risk of data breach and regulatory penalties → Systemic: External collaboration capacity increases by 40%, while on-site governance costs rise by 15%; trade-off: collaboration velocity vs privacy burden → Strategic: The Bonn unit becomes a privacy-forward research hub enabling broader multi-site parasomnia phenotyping, but requires sustained governance investments and may slow initial onboarding.
options: Tighten consent governance and enforce de-identification, storing data on-site with strict access controls.
Establish data-use agreements with external collaborators and implement quarterly privacy audits.
Implement privacy-preserving federated analytics with a data-access licensing model that monetizes de-identified insights while keeping raw data local.
review: Controls privacy safeguards vs. external collaboration opportunities. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border data transfer regulations for international collaborations.

name: Event-Triage Automation Maturation
consequences: Immediate: Reduces manual adjudication workload and accelerates preliminary event classification → Systemic: Annotation throughput increases by 60% and misclassification declines by 12%, while onboarding new raters requires 20% more training time; trade-off: speed vs. accuracy → Strategic: Enables scaling to multi-site studies and frees researchers for deeper analyses, but requires ongoing model governance and data quality assurance.
options: Sustain manual adjudication by two independent raters with regular reliability audits.
Introduce machine-learning assisted triage that flags probable events for human review.
Pilot federated learning-enabled triage across Bonn and partner labs to reduce manual effort while maintaining human-in-the-loop verification.
review: Controls manual adjudication workload vs. automation-driven efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider model bias and false negatives introduced by automation.

name: Residential Safety Envelope Optimization
consequences: Immediate: Enhances occupant safety and reduces incident risk → Systemic: Incident rate falls by 25%, maintenance costs rise by 20%, and energy use increases by 5%; trade-off: safety vs cost and comfort → Strategic: Position Bonn as a safety-forward lab with better risk management, but requiring ongoing budget commitments.
options: Maintain current safety features and domestic ambiance to preserve participant comfort.
Upgrade detection systems with enhanced door alarms, padded edges, and real-time incident dashboards.
Deploy dynamic safety architecture with smart occupancy controls, rapid-response drills, and vendor-managed safety services under a fixed annual program.
review: Controls safety performance vs participant comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider staff fatigue and overtime costs from extended safety protocols.

name: EEG-BIDS Data Infrastructure Modernization
consequences: Immediate: Improves data consistency and auditability → Systemic: Data latency improves by 40%, storage and compute costs rise by 12%; trade-off: interoperability vs cost → Strategic: Builds capacity for cross-site comparative analyses, but requires robust IT governance to avoid vendor lock-in.
options: Standardize EEG-BIDS annotation pipeline with automated labeling and batch export.
Integrate on-prem NAS with encrypted cloud backups and periodic integrity checks.
Adopt privacy-preserving cross-center analytics via edge compute and cross-site federated sharing with regional data governance.
review: Controls data interoperability vs storage/compute costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider vendor lock-in risk and data governance complexities over time.

name: Participant Recruitment and Retention Protocols
consequences: Immediate: Boosts enrollment interest → Systemic: Enrollment rate increases by 25%, dropout rate decreases by 15%, per-participant costs rise by 10%; trade-off: recruitment speed vs budget efficiency → Strategic: Improves cohort representativeness and data completeness; costs escalate and require budget planning.
options: Increase per-night compensation to 100€ and streamline pre-admission screening.
Offer flexible stay durations with modular engagement and community liaison support.
Implement targeted clinician referrals and adaptive recruitment scoring to reduce dropouts.
review: Controls recruitment speed vs representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential bias from compensation across socio-economic groups.

name: Funding Diversification and Governance
consequences: Immediate: Diversifies funding sources and reduces reliance on a single funder → Systemic: Funding volatility decreases by 30%, industry partnerships add 8% overhead and require governance; trade-off: independence vs revenue → Strategic: Stabilizes finances and broadens the research network; risk: misalignment with academic priorities if funding is industry-driven.
options: Pursue national and EU grants with milestone-driven budgeting.
Engage industry partners with milestone-based funding while preserving publication rights.
Create a controlled-access research consortium with member institutions paying for data access.
review: Controls funding stability vs academic independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory constraints on industry-funded research and publication rights across partners.
